is a British [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") founded by Jamaican [Duke Reid](/wiki/Duke_Reid "Duke Reid"), [Lee Gopthal](/wiki/Lee_Gopthal "Lee Gopthal") and [Chris Blackwell](/wiki/Chris_Blackwell "Chris Blackwell") in 1968. It specialises in [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [dub music](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music"). The label currently operates under the [Sanctuary Records](/wiki/Sanctuary_Records "Sanctuary Records") Group. The name *Trojan* comes from the [Croydon](/wiki/Croydon "Croydon")-built [Trojan truck](/wiki/Trojan_(automobile) "Trojan (automobile)") that was used as Duke Reid's [sound system](/wiki/Sound_system_(Jamaican) "Sound system (Jamaican)") in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"). The truck had "Duke Reid - The Trojan King of Sounds" painted on the sides, and the music played by Reid became known as the *Trojan Sound*.

["BMG forms alliance with Universal Music Group; plans to move physical distribution to UMG's Commercial Services division"](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/bmg-forms-alliance-with-universal-sets-plan-to-move-physical-distro-to-umgs-commercial-services-division12/). *musicbusinessworldwide.com*. 19 October 2023.

Record label

| Trojan Records | |
| --- | --- |
| Parent company | [Sanctuary Records Group](/wiki/Sanctuary_Records "Sanctuary Records") ([BMG Rights Management](/wiki/BMG_Rights_Management "BMG Rights Management")) |
| Founded | 1968 |
| Founder | [Lee Gopthal](/wiki/Lee_Gopthal "Lee Gopthal"), [Duke Reid](/wiki/Duke_Reid "Duke Reid"), [Chris Blackwell](/wiki/Chris_Blackwell "Chris Blackwell") |
| Distributors | [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group")[[1]](#cite_note-1) (physical)   [BMG Rights Management](/wiki/BMG_Rights_Management "BMG Rights Management") (digital) |
| Genre | * [Ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") * [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") * [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") * [dub](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music") * [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall") * [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") |
| Country of origin | [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") |
| Official website | [trojanrecords.com](https://www.trojanrecords.com/) |

**Trojan Records** is a British [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") founded by Jamaican [Duke Reid](/wiki/Duke_Reid "Duke Reid"), [Lee Gopthal](/wiki/Lee_Gopthal "Lee Gopthal") and [Chris Blackwell](/wiki/Chris_Blackwell "Chris Blackwell") in 1968. It specialises in [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [dub music](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music"). The label currently operates under the [Sanctuary Records](/wiki/Sanctuary_Records "Sanctuary Records") Group. The name *Trojan* comes from the [Croydon](/wiki/Croydon "Croydon")-built [Trojan truck](/wiki/Trojan_(automobile) "Trojan (automobile)") that was used as Duke Reid's [sound system](/wiki/Sound_system_(Jamaican) "Sound system (Jamaican)") in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"). The truck had "Duke Reid - The Trojan King of Sounds" painted on the sides, and the music played by Reid became known as the *Trojan Sound*.

The label had almost 30 hit singles in the [UK singles chart](/wiki/UK_singles_chart "UK singles chart") between 1969 and 1976.[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trojan_Records&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Trojan Records was founded in 1968 when [Lee Gopthal](/wiki/Lee_Gopthal "Lee Gopthal"), who operated the Musicland record retail chain and owned [Beat & Commercial Records](/wiki/B%26C_Records "B&C Records"), pooled his Jamaican music interests with those of [Chris Blackwell](/wiki/Chris_Blackwell "Chris Blackwell")'s [Island Records](/wiki/Island_Records "Island Records").[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) Until 1975, they were based at a warehouse in Neasden Lane, [Willesden](/wiki/Willesden "Willesden"), London.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Trojan was instrumental in introducing reggae to a global audience and, by 1970, had secured a series of major UK chart hits. Successful Trojan artists from this period include [Judge Dread](/wiki/Judge_Dread "Judge Dread"), [Tony Tribe](/wiki/Tony_Tribe "Tony Tribe"), [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry")'s Upsetters, [Bob and Marcia](/wiki/Bob_and_Marcia "Bob and Marcia"), [Desmond Dekker](/wiki/Desmond_Dekker "Desmond Dekker"), [Jimmy Cliff](/wiki/Jimmy_Cliff "Jimmy Cliff"), the [Harry J](/wiki/Harry_J "Harry J") All Stars, [the Maytals](/wiki/The_Maytals "The Maytals"), [the Melodians](/wiki/The_Melodians "The Melodians"), [Nicky Thomas](/wiki/Nicky_Thomas_(singer) "Nicky Thomas (singer)") and [Dave and Ansel Collins](/wiki/Dave_and_Ansel_Collins "Dave and Ansel Collins").

The bulk of the company's successes came via licences for [Jamaican music](/wiki/Music_of_Jamaica "Music of Jamaica") supplied by producers such as [Duke Reid](/wiki/Duke_Reid "Duke Reid"), [Harry Johnson](/wiki/Harry_J "Harry J") and [Leslie Kong](/wiki/Leslie_Kong "Leslie Kong").[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) While the company's focus was firmly on the sale of 7” singles, it also launched a series of popular, budget-priced compilations such as *Tighten Up*, *Club Reggae* and *Reggae Chartbusters*.[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2)

In 1972, Chris Blackwell decided to withdraw [Island Records](/wiki/Island_Records "Island Records") interests in Trojan.[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) By this time, the company was trying to broaden the appeal of reggae by [re-mastering](/wiki/Audio_mastering "Audio mastering") and [overdubbing](/wiki/Overdubbing "Overdubbing") string arrangements on the original recordings.[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) While the move initially paid dividends, the costs involved began to take their toll on the company's finances. In 1975, the company was liquidated and its assets acquired by Marcel Rodd's Saga Records, which had previously largely focused on releasing budget [LPs](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record").[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2)

In 1985, record collector and accountant [Colin Newman](/wiki/Colin_Newman "Colin Newman") purchased Trojan Records.[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) Over the years that ensued, the company mainly concentrated on re-releasing much of its back catalogue, issuing numerous ska, rocksteady and reggae recordings.[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) Among their most popular releases were the [Trojan Box Set series](/wiki/Trojan_Box_Set_series "Trojan Box Set series"), each featuring 50 songs on a three-CD (or vinyl record) set in a "clamshell" box. In addition, Trojan licensed reggae classics for a number of TV commercial campaigns, most notably for [TDK](/wiki/TDK "TDK"), [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") and the [2000 UEFA European Football Championship](/wiki/2000_UEFA_European_Football_Championship "2000 UEFA European Football Championship").

The [Sanctuary Records](/wiki/Sanctuary_Records "Sanctuary Records") Group purchased Trojan Records in 2001 and continued to focus on the label's back catalogue, under the general management of [Laurence Cane-Honeysett](/wiki/Laurence_Cane-Honeysett "Laurence Cane-Honeysett"). In 2002, Trojan's new owners acquired former UK rival record company, [Creole Records](/wiki/Creole_Records "Creole Records"), previously owned by Bruce White and Gary Himmelfarb's US-based [RAS Records](/wiki/RAS_Records "RAS Records"). In August 2007, [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") (UMG) had acquired 90% of Sanctuary Records after announcing in June a share offer that valued Sanctuary at $US87.68 million.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 2013, UMG sold their entire Sanctuary back catalogue including Trojan to [BMG](/wiki/BMG_Rights_Management "BMG Rights Management"), as part of a divestment programme that was a mandatory condition set by EU regulators when UMG acquired EMI in 2012.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 2018, the company celebrated 50 years in the business.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Influence on skinheads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trojan_Records&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Influence on skinheads")]

[Trojan skinheads](/wiki/Trojan_skinhead "Trojan skinhead"), influenced by traditional 1960s [skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") culture, are named after Trojan Records, to stress the influence of [black](/wiki/Black_people "Black people") [Jamaican music](/wiki/Music_of_Jamaica "Music of Jamaica") and the [rude boy](/wiki/Rude_boy "Rude boy") style to the skinhead [subculture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture"). This designation emphasizes differences from the [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock")-influenced [Oi!](/wiki/Oi! "Oi!") skinheads of the 1980s. The [logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo") of [Skinheads Against Racial Prejudice](/wiki/Skinheads_Against_Racial_Prejudice "Skinheads Against Racial Prejudice") (SHARP) is based on the Trojan Records logo, although the [Corinthian helmet](/wiki/Corinthian_helmet "Corinthian helmet") is reversed to face the opposite direction. It was designed by Roddy Moreno of antifascist Oi! band [The Oppressed](/wiki/The_Oppressed "The Oppressed"). "I reversed the Trojan helmet because SHARP, in the plume, looked better that way."[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

## Collectable records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trojan_Records&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Collectable records")]

Over the years; as demand for many rare records has increased, a number of Trojan releases have become collector's items. For example, a 1969 single by The Slickers entitled "Run Fattie" sold for more than £300 in 2015.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Other collectable records on Trojan that have sold for £200 or more include "Night Of Love" by Ansel Collins,[[8]](#cite_note-8) "Wiggle Waggle" by The Wanderers,[[9]](#cite_note-9) and "Hang 'Em High" by Richard Ace.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Further reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trojan_Records&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Further reading")]
